commonFindings.info: Disc herniations often at the L4-L5 or L5-S1 levels.

commonFindings.title: Symptoms of radiating leg pain or sciatica, especially with nerve root compression
commonFindings.info: Symptoms of radiating leg pain or sciatica, especially with nerve root compression

commonlyMissed.title: Facet joint osteoarthritis
commonlyMissed.info: Facet joint osteoarthritis: Osteoarthritic changes in the facet joints can also cause back pain and are sometimes overshadowed by the more obvious disc pathologies on MRI.

commonlyMissed.title: Annular tears
commonlyMissed.info: Annular tears: Small tears in the annulus fibrosus (outer layer of the disc) can cause significant pain but might not always be evident on standard MR sequences.

commonlyMissed.title: Sacroiliac joint dysfunction
commonlyMissed.info: Sacroiliac joint dysfunction: Pain from the sacroiliac joints can mimic lumbar spine pathologies. While the lumbar MRI might look at the lowermost part of the sacroiliac joints, it might miss subtle changes.

commonlyMissed.title: Modic changes
commonlyMissed.info: Modic changes: These are bone marrow changes adjacent to degenerated discs that can be a source of chronic low back pain but might be overlooked if not specifically sought.

commonlyMissed.title: Piriformis syndrome
commonlyMissed.info: Piriformis syndrome: Although it is a muscular cause, piriformis syndrome can mimic a herniated disc. While it's best diagnosed clinically, it's often missed if one is solely focused on radiographic findings.

commonlyMissed.title: Adjacent segment disease
commonlyMissed.info: Adjacent segment disease: If a patient had prior lumbar surgery, sometimes the segments above or below the operated level might develop pathologies that can be subtle initially.
